sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Lumbar radiculopathy is leg pain and neurological irritation arising from the lumbar spine. The main task is differentiating uncomplicated radicular pain from cauda equina, progressive deficit, or major non-spinal mimics.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Leg pain below the knee, often worse than back pain
- Paresthesia or numbness in a dermatomal pattern
- Possible myotomal weakness or reduced reflexes
- Pain provoked by coughing, sneezing, or neural tension

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Screen for cauda equina symptoms, progressive weakness, bilateral symptoms, severe night pain, infection, cancer, or recent major trauma
- Not all leg pain is radiculopathy; consider vascular, hip, or peripheral nerve causes
- Severe pain alone does not always equal urgent surgery, but progressive deficit raises urgency

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Screen neurological status, bladder and bowel red flags, and safe mobility
- Encourage comfortable movement and simple symptom relief within scope
- Escalate urgently if cauda equina features or progressive motor loss are present

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Perform focused neurological and neural-tension examination
- Use graded loading, nerve-friendly movement advice, and positional modification where appropriate
- Avoid repeated aggressive stretching into severe nerve irritation
- Refer for urgent imaging or specialist review if red flags, severe functional decline, or progressive deficit are present

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Clear documentation of motor findings and any change over time is important
